critique.summary: The article introduces purl, a command-line interface tool similar to curl, designed to make HTTP requests that require payment. Developed by Stripe, purl allows users to interact with paid endpoints by integrating payment functionality directly into HTTP requests.
mainPoints: purl is a CLI tool inspired by curl for making HTTP requests that require payment.
It is built by Stripe and the source code is available on GitHub.
Users can install purl via Homebrew or a shell script.
purl supports wallet setup and can handle both free and paid HTTP endpoints, with an example of a paid endpoint costing 0.01 USDC.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about purl being a curl-like CLI tool for paid HTTP requests built by Stripe are accurate based on the official purl.dev website and Stripe's announcements. The installation methods and usage examples correspond with the documentation provided by Stripe. There is no indication of misinformation or exaggeration in the article.
considerations: The article does not mention potential security or privacy implications of using a payment-enabled HTTP client.
There is no discussion of supported payment methods beyond USDC or how payments are processed and verified.
The article lacks information on the scope of use cases or limitations of purl in real-world applications.
No mention is made of compatibility with existing curl scripts or integration with other tools.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the introduction of HTTP 402 Payment Required in a new CLI tool called purl, with skepticism about its practical adoption and concerns about technical issues and security. Some users question the clarity of the marketing and the reliability of the tool in real-world scenarios.

comments.author: acheong08
comments.text:
A couple questions:

- The default seems to make the payment without confirmation. What stops an endpoint from changing payment amount between an inspect request and the actual request?

- Will adoption of this payment protocol ever grow large enough for anyone to implement this on either the client or server?

- Bots have more of a financial incentive to crawl sites than a human. I doubt this will actually stop anything

- I see a AGENTS.md. How much of this is vibe coded? It's near impossible to get a sense of the care taken to review LLM output. Hard to trust with money.
comments.analysis: This comment raises valid, detailed concerns about the protocol's security, adoption feasibility, and trustworthiness, reflecting a deep understanding of the issues involved.

comments.author: connorgurney
comments.text: It only took us 29 years but HTTP 402 Payment Required might finally mean something on a wider scale…
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully notes the historical context of the HTTP 402 status code and its potential newfound relevance, though it is more observational than analytical.

comments.author: rtpg
comments.text:
Uncharacteristically unclear marketing from Stripe!

You're gonna have to give me more to go off of than this.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses frustration about unclear marketing, which is a reasonable critique but lacks technical depth or constructive suggestions.

comments.author: gforce_de
comments.text:
user@NAS:~$ curl -fsSl https://www.purl.dev/install.sh | bash
  ...
  purl: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.38' not found (required by purl)
  
  user@NAS:~$ uname -a
  Linux NAS 6.1.0-43-amd64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.1.162-1 (2026-02-08) x86_64 GNU/Linux
comments.analysis: This comment provides a concrete example of a technical problem encountered, useful for understanding deployment challenges, though it is limited to a single environment.
